title: Massive Darkness - Review
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GYD-rQNG-rI

Daniel (@dungeondive) reviews Massive Darkness and comes out, against the grain, a fan — he thinks CMON's dungeon crawler gets an unfairly bad rap. His central thesis is a cautionary tale about listening to a crowd: the game was conceived as a snappy zero-to-hero, one-and-done crawl, but Kickstarter backers demanded a campaign, and the bolted-on (and weak) campaign system diluted the original focus.

Where it shines is at the micro level. Heroes pair a character with one of a dozen class booklets full of skills, and the loot flows at a glorious Diablo pace — a dozen items cycled through in roughly three tiles and an hour and a quarter of solo play, levelling two heroes from zero to three. Best of all, monsters don't just drop loot; if the drawn treasure's symbols match, they actively wield it against you, which he loves. The rulebook is the usual CMON gold standard.

Where it falls apart is the map: the tiles are inert, with nothing to search or interact with beyond a light/dark shadow-mode wrinkle, and the much-hoped-for event deck is a disappointment — no narrative, just heal-or-spawn mechanics. Quests are Zombicide-style fetch errands. His verdict: genuinely fun, now effectively a dead game, so grab it cheap with the Kickstarter extras.

----

Massive Darkness divides people sharply — where do you land, and does a fast Diablo-style loot rush outweigh a lifeless map for you?
